name: Pierce & Kwok LLP
description_short: NYC-based law firm specializing in business, commercial litigation, and bankruptcy services for entrepreneurs, artists, startups, and small to midsize companies across multiple states.
description_long:
Pierce & Kwok LLP is a New York City-based law firm founded to serve entrepreneurs, performing and visual artists, small businesses, startups, and midsize companies. The firm positions itself as an accessible, client-focused practice that guides businesses through every lifecycle stage from incubation and startup through growth, financing rounds, mergers, acquisitions, and IPO events. According to their website, they maintain a verified Chamber of Commerce membership and hold ratings from Super Lawyers and Avvo platforms.

The firm offers a broad spectrum of legal services including bankruptcy representation, commercial and civil litigation, business transactional work, entertainment law, intellectual property protection, hospitality and liquor license law, and contract drafting and negotiation. They serve clients across New York, New Jersey, California, and beyond. Their track record includes hundreds of successful commercial and criminal litigations, numerous patent and trademark acquisitions, representation of over 200 bars, restaurants, music venues and art galleries, and countless contract negotiations.

Pierce & Kwok differentiates itself through an emphasis on accessibility and client service orientation. The website highlights that attorneys provide free initial consultations, describe themselves as "generous with their time," and emphasize "plain-speaking practicality." Partner Aaron Pierce has appeared on the "Lawyers Who Care" podcast discussing client-focused service beyond standard legal counsel. The firm explicitly targets creative professionals and the entertainment/hospitality sectors where they claim specialized expertise.

While the website provides no specific information about bankruptcy filing volumes, success rates, fee structures, or client outcomes, the firm's positioning as a general business law practice with bankruptcy listed as one service among many suggests this may not be their primary specialization. Potential clients should clarify bankruptcy experience levels and whether the firm's strength in entertainment and hospitality law translates to bankruptcy representation.
pros: Free initial consultations available to prospective clients
Verified Chamber of Commerce membership with Super Lawyers and Avvo ratings
Demonstrated track record of 200+ bars, restaurants, music venues and art galleries represented
Multistate practice covering NY, NJ, CA and beyond
Partner Aaron Pierce featured on 'Lawyers Who Care' podcast for client advocacy
Specialized expertise in entertainment and hospitality law sectors
Hundreds of completed commercial and criminal litigations documented
cons: Website provides no specific information about bankruptcy filing experience, Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 specialization, or bankruptcy case outcomes
Bankruptcy appears as one service among many (commercial litigation, entertainment law, IP law), suggesting it may not be a primary practice focus
No bankruptcy-specific fee structure, payment plans, or pricing transparency disclosed
No client testimonials or case results specific to bankruptcy representation
Limited detail on whether firm handles personal bankruptcy, business bankruptcy, or both
best_for: Entrepreneurs and small business owners in entertainment, hospitality, and creative industries seeking general business counsel including bankruptcy options
NYC-based startup founders and midsize companies needing integrated legal services across multiple practice areas
Artists, performers, and creative professionals with complex business structures requiring specialized legal guidance
Bar, restaurant, music venue and art gallery owners facing insolvency or business restructuring
diagnosis: Pierce & Kwok LLP is best suited for entrepreneurs and small business owners in creative, entertainment, and hospitality sectors who need comprehensive business legal services with bankruptcy as one available option. The primary caveat is that bankruptcy appears to be a secondary service within a broader business law practice rather than a core specialization, so clients should verify the firm's specific bankruptcy experience and success rates before retaining them for insolvency matters.
